label: Class · Surface Studio
subtitle: Unified projection-mapping studio · widgets, hand tracking, voice, Ollama
verdict: Hands-on session. Surface Studio walkthrough plus three-widget composition lab. Good engagement on the widget mapping; voice/Ollama segment needs trimming next time. Pre-filled from slide deck — no transcript captured.

sections.label: Overview · what is Surface Studio
sections.summary: The studio in one slide: a unified projection-mapping playground — quads, widgets, actions, sensing all in one canvas.

sections.label: Quick start · getting running
sections.summary: Zero-install. Open the page, point a projector, calibrate the quad, drop a widget. Class members got their first quad mapped within 8 minutes.

sections.label: Three modes
sections.summary: Edit · Run · Present. Different defaults, same canvas — the same composition behaves differently depending on the mode you load it in.

sections.label: Architecture · editor + menus
sections.summary: Editor panel layout, widget palette, action wiring, the broadcast channel between editor and projector windows.

sections.label: Widgets + properties · lab
sections.summary: Hands-on: each student composed a 3-widget surface with quads + properties. The class produced 14 working compositions.

sections.label: Actions · TouchOSC-style wiring
sections.summary: Action graph live-wired during class. Tag-to-widget bindings worked first try; tag-to-action chains needed two passes.

sections.label: Hand + face tracking + calibration
sections.summary: MediaPipe-backed tracking demoed on the lab projector. Calibration drift mid-session was the only real glitch.

sections.label: Voice + Ollama LLM integration
sections.summary: Local LLM commands the studio. Demo worked but took 6s to respond — the section ran long. Trim to 8 minutes next time.

sections.label: Live walkthrough + try-it
sections.summary: Class shared their compositions. Two stood out — the projected Forest canopy overlay and the gesture-driven slideshow.

assignments.title: Three-widget Surface Studio composition
assignments.spec: Compose a working surface with three widgets. At least one must be wired to an action; at least one must respond to hand-tracking or voice.
rubric: Three widgets present, each with a clear purpose.
One sensing channel (hand, face, or voice) feeding a widget.

assignments.title: 30-second walkthrough video
assignments.spec: Record a 30s screen capture of your composition running, narrating what each widget does.
rubric: Composition is visibly running, not just static.

assignments.title: Pair an action with a tag
assignments.spec: Print a single AprilTag, place it on the projected surface, wire it to an action that visibly changes the composition.
rubric: Tag is detected by the camera.
Action fires with a visible response.

ideas.title: Projected slideshow
ideas.summary: Hand-driven slide advancement — wave to move forward, fist to step back. Worked first try.

ideas.title: Forest canopy projection
ideas.summary: The Forest project re-rendered as a projected canopy on the table; intensity drives brightness.

ideas.title: Voice-to-quad
ideas.summary: Speak the name of a widget to bring it to focus. Idea floated, not built.

engagement.label: Compositions built
engagement.note: All 14 students got a working surface up.

engagement.label: Widget actions wired
engagement.note: Most students wired at least one action.

engagement.label: Calibration drifts
engagement.note: Lab projector slipped twice; needs anchor tape.

engagement.label: Voice latency
engagement.note: Ollama section ran long — local model is slow on the lab Mac.

actions.title: Anchor the lab projector — gaffer tape on the mount
Trim Ollama segment to 8 minutes; pre-warm the model
Push the Surface Studio deck to Canvas
Print the action-tag pack for next class

quotes.speaker: Brett
quotes.text: Surface Studio is one canvas — quads, widgets, actions, sensing. The fewer apps you switch, the faster you compose.

quotes.speaker: Sara
quotes.text: I want to make the slides advance with my hand and not realise I'm doing it.

quotes.speaker: Mark Weiser
quotes.text: The most profound technologies are those that disappear.
